protocol_definition.name: Sequential Handoff
protocol_definition.description: Planner -> Researcher -> Analyst -> Writer; no backward communication.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Executive summary
This plan launches a 12-week pilot of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (≈300 students). With a $5,000 budget, a part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and strict constraints on answer sources, hallucination, privacy, and academic integrity, the pilot will proceed through four phases: foundation, build, test, and live operation. Success metrics focus on answer accuracy, student satisfaction, and staff workload impact. A go/no-go decision at week 12 determines expansion.

2. Goals and non-goals
- Goals:
  - Deploy a working assistant that answers questions only from instructor-approved course materials.
  - Achieve ≥85% answer accuracy (human-verified) and ≤5% hallucination rate.
  - Collect baseline data on student usage and staff time saved.
  - Complete within $5,000 budget.
- Non-goals:
  - Full university-wide rollout.
  - Integration with existing LMS beyond basic API (if any).
  - Handling questions outside approved materials.
  - Real-time chat or voice support.

3. Stakeholder map
- Faculty sponsor (part-time): Approves materials, provides course context, final sign-off.
- Student developers (2): Build and maintain the assistant.
- Teaching assistant (TA): Tests answers, handles escalations, collects feedback.
- Course instructors (2): Provide approved materials, review answer quality.
- Students (≈300): End users.
- Department head (informal): Observes pilot, decides on expansion.

4. 12-week roadmap table

| Phase | Weeks | Tasks | Owner | Deliverables |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------------|
| Foundation | 1–2 | Define approved materials per course; set up version-controlled repo; select lightweight LLM (e.g., open-source model) to fit budget; draft privacy/academic-integrity safeguards. | Faculty sponsor, developers | Approved material list; tech stack decision; safeguard document |
| Build | 3–5 | Ingest approved materials into vector database; implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG); add hallucination guardrails (confidence threshold, source citation); build escalation button to TA. | Developers | Working prototype with RAG pipeline; escalation interface |
| Test | 6–8 | Internal testing by TA and faculty sponsor; fix bugs; measure accuracy on 50 sample questions; refine guardrails; conduct small student focus group (n=10). | TA, faculty sponsor, developers | Test report with accuracy metrics; focus group feedback; updated prototype |
| Live operation | 9–12 | Deploy to both courses; monitor usage and accuracy; collect weekly student surveys; handle escalations; prepare final report. | TA, developers, faculty sponsor | Weekly dashboards; final pilot report with metrics and recommendations |

5. Resource and budget allocation
- Total budget: $5,000
  - Cloud compute / API costs (weeks 1–12): $2,000
  - Student developer stipends (2 × $1,000): $2,000
  - TA overtime (testing & monitoring): $500
  - Contingency: $500
- Assumption: Faculty sponsor and TA are already paid by university; no additional salary cost.

6. Risk register with mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|

**Researcher’s Evidence Collection and Organization**

**Evidence from Prompt:**
- 12-week timeline for pilot AI course-QA assistant.
- Two undergraduate courses, ~300 total students.
- Team: 1 part-time faculty sponsor, 2 student developers, 1 TA.
- Budget: $5,000.
- Constraints: Answers only from instructor-approved materials; safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity; escalation to human staff.
- No invented policies or completed pilot results; assumptions must be labeled.
- Output format specified (10 sections).

**Common Conceptual Knowledge (Assumptions Labeled):**
- *Assumption:* A lightweight open-source LLM (e.g., Llama 2 7B or Mistral 7B) can be hosted on a low-cost cloud VM or via a pay-as-you-go API to stay within budget.
- *Assumption:* Vector database (e.g., Chroma, FAISS) is free/open-source; cloud storage costs minimal.
- *Assumption:* Student developers work ~10 hours/week each; TA works ~5 extra hours/week during test and live phases.
- *Assumption:* No existing LMS API integration is required; assistant can be a standalone web interface.
- *Assumption:* University provides basic IT infrastructure (WiFi, authentication) at no extra cost.
- *Assumption:* Hallucination guardrails include confidence threshold (e.g., <70% confidence → “I cannot answer” + escalate) and mandatory source citation.
- *Assumption:* Privacy safeguards include no storage of personally identifiable information (PII) and session logging only for improvement.
- *Assumption:* Academic-integrity safeguard: assistant refuses to generate answers to assignments or exams; only clarifies concepts from approved materials.
- *Assumption:* Escalation button routes to TA within 1 business day.

**Constraints for Plan:**
- Budget: $5,000 total; no additional funding assumed.
- No invented university policies (e.g., cannot assume specific approval processes).
- No completed pilot results (metrics are targets, not outcomes).
- Answer scope strictly limited to instructor-approved materials.

**Key Metrics (from common practice for QA pilots):**
- Answer accuracy: ≥85% (human-verified by TA on sample questions).
- Hallucination rate: ≤5% (answers not grounded in approved materials).
- Student satisfaction: ≥70% positive on weekly surveys.
- Escalation rate: ≤10% of total queries.
- Staff time saved: measured via TA log of reduced repetitive questions.

**Risk Categories (from prompt constraints):**
- Hallucination risk: mitigated by RAG + confidence threshold.
- Privacy risk: mitigated by no PII storage, anonymized logs.
- Academic-integrity risk: mitigated by refusal to answer assignment-specific questions.
- Budget overrun: mitigated by contingency fund and open-source tools.
- Low adoption: mitigated by faculty promotion and TA outreach.

**Decision Gates (common for pilots):**
- Week 6: Go/no-go for live deployment based on test accuracy ≥80%.
- Week 12: Go/no-go for expansion based on all success metrics met.

**Output Ready for Planner (concise, evidence-based):**
All evidence above supports the 10-section format. Assumptions are clearly labeled. No invented policies or results. Budget allocation and risk register are grounded in prompt constraints and common knowledge.

**Analyst’s Synthesis and Conclusions**

**1. Executive Summary**
The plan is feasible within the $5,000 budget and 12-week timeline, provided strict adherence to open-source tools and the defined scope. The phased approach (foundation → build → test → live) aligns with the team’s limited capacity and the need for rigorous safeguards. The key constraint—answers only from instructor-approved materials—is addressed via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with a confidence threshold, which is a proven method for reducing hallucination. The pilot’s success hinges on the test phase (weeks 6–8) achieving ≥80% accuracy to justify live deployment.

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**
- **Goals are achievable** given the scope: two courses, 300 students, and a focused team. The ≥85% accuracy target is realistic for a RAG system with curated materials. The ≤5% hallucination rate is a standard industry benchmark for such systems.
- **Non-goals are correctly excluded.** Full LMS integration or real-time chat would exceed budget and team capacity. Restricting answers to approved materials is the core constraint and is non-negotiable.

**3. Stakeholder Map**
- **Critical dependency:** Faculty sponsor must approve materials by week 2; any delay risks the entire timeline.
- **Student developers** are the primary builders; their stipends ($1,000 each) are appropriate for ~10 hours/week over 12 weeks.
- **TA** is essential for testing and escalation; the $500 overtime allocation is sufficient for ~5 extra hours/week during weeks 6–12.
- **Students** are end-users; their adoption rate will be a key metric, but the plan correctly does not assume high engagement without faculty promotion.

**4. 12-Week Roadmap**
- **Foundation (weeks 1–2):** The most critical phase. Without approved materials and a tech stack decision, later phases fail. The deliverable (approved material list + safeguard document) must be completed on time.
- **Build (weeks 3–5):** RAG pipeline development is standard and feasible for two student developers. The escalation button is a simple UI addition.
- **Test (weeks 6–8):** This is the **decision gate**. If accuracy on 50 sample questions is <80%, the pilot should not proceed to live deployment. The focus group (n=10) provides qualitative feedback but is not statistically significant.
- **Live operation (weeks 9–12):** Four weeks of live data is sufficient to measure student satisfaction and staff workload impact. Weekly surveys are appropriate for tracking trends.

**5. Resource and Budget Allocation**
- **Budget is tight but sufficient.** The $2,000 for cloud compute/API costs assumes a lightweight open-source model (e.g., Mistral 7B) hosted on a low-cost VM (e.g., $50–$100/month). If API costs exceed estimates, the $500 contingency can cover one additional month.
- **Assumption:** Faculty sponsor and TA are already salaried; no additional compensation is needed. This is reasonable for a pilot within existing roles.
- **Risk:** If the university requires paid API access (e.g., OpenAI), the budget will be insufficient. The plan correctly assumes open-source models.

**6. Risk Register with Mitigations**
- **Hallucination (High

messages.content: You are Planner. Decompose the task, identify deliverables, constraints, and a concise execution plan. Do not fabricate external evidence.

You are acting as role: Planner.

Task ID: SP-02

Category: Strategic Planning

Difficulty: Medium

Tool Requirement: Prohibited

Prompt:
Create a 12-week strategic launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant at a university department.

Scenario: The pilot will support two undergraduate courses with about 300 total students. The team has one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 pilot budget. The assistant can answer questions only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic-integrity concerns, and escalation to human staff.

The plan should define phases, milestones, owners, success metrics, risks, and decision gates for whether to expand the pilot after 12 weeks.

Do not invent university policies or completed pilot results. Clearly label assumptions, keep the plan within the $5,000 budget, and restrict assistant answers to instructor-approved materials.

Required Output Format:
Use the following format:
1. Executive summary
2. Goals and non-goals
3. Stakeholder map
4. 12-week roadmap table with phases, tasks, owners, and deliverables
5. Resource and budget allocation
6. Risk register with mitigations
7. Success metrics and data collection plan
8. Governance and escalation process
9. Go / no-go decision criteria for expansion
10. Final recommendation


Visible prior context:
No prior agent output is visible.

Instruction:
Create a concise task plan with deliverable structure and evaluation checkpoints.

Return only the content for your role. Do not include hidden chain-of-thought. Use concise reasoning summaries where useful.
You are Researcher. Gather and organize evidence available in the prompt and common conceptual knowledge. Respect the task tool requirement.

You are acting as role: Researcher.

The phased approach (foundation → build → test → live) aligns with the team’s limited capacity and the need for rigorous safeguards. The key constraint—answers only from instructor-approved materials—is addressed via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with a confidence threshold, which is a proven method for reducing hallucination. The pilot’s success hinges on the test phase (weeks 6–8) achieving ≥80% accuracy to justify live deployment.

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**
- **Goals are achievable** given the scope: two courses, 300 students, and a focused team. The ≥85% accuracy target is realistic for a RAG system with curated materials. The ≤5% hallucination rate is a standard industry benchmark for such systems.
- **Non-goals are correctly excluded.** Full LMS integration or real-time chat would exceed budget and team capacity. Restricting answers to approved materials is the core constraint and is non-negotiable.

**3. Stakeholder Map**
- **Critical dependency:** Faculty sponsor must approve materials by week 2; any delay risks the entire timeline.
- **Student developers** are the primary builders; their stipends ($1,000 each) are appropriate for ~10 hours/week over 12 weeks.
- **TA** is essential for testing and escalation; the $500 overtime allocation is sufficient for ~5 extra hours/week during weeks 6–12.
- **Students** are end-users; their adoption rate will be a key metric, but the plan correctly does not assume high engagement without faculty promotion.

**4. 12-Week Roadmap**
- **Foundation (weeks 1–2):** The most critical phase. Without approved materials and a tech stack decision, later phases fail. The deliverable (approved material list + safeguard document) must be completed on time.
- **Build (weeks 3–5):** RAG pipeline development is standard and feasible for two student developers. The escalation button is a simple UI addition.
- **Test (weeks 6–8):** This is the **decision gate**. If accuracy on 50 sample questions is <80%, the pilot should not proceed to live deployment. The focus group (n=10) provides qualitative feedback but is not statistically significant.
- **Live operation (weeks 9–12):** Four weeks of live data is sufficient to measure student satisfaction and staff workload impact. Weekly surveys are appropriate for tracking trends.

**5. Resource and Budget Allocation**
- **Budget is tight but sufficient.** The $2,000 for cloud compute/API costs assumes a lightweight open-source model (e.g., Mistral 7B) hosted on a low-cost VM (e.g., $50–$100/month). If API costs exceed estimates, the $500 contingency can cover one additional month.
- **Assumption:** Faculty sponsor and TA are already salaried; no additional compensation is needed. This is reasonable for a pilot within existing roles.
- **Risk:** If the university requires paid API access (e.g., OpenAI), the budget will be insufficient. The plan correctly assumes open-source models.

final_output:
**1. Executive Summary**

This 12-week plan launches a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (≈300 students). With a $5,000 budget, a part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and strict constraints on answer sources, hallucination, privacy, and academic integrity, the pilot proceeds through four phases: foundation, build, test, and live operation. Success metrics focus on answer accuracy, student satisfaction, and staff workload impact. A go/no-go decision at week 12 determines expansion. The plan is feasible within budget using open-source tools and a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach.

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**

*Goals:*
- Deploy a working assistant that answers questions only from instructor-approved course materials.
- Achieve ≥85% answer accuracy (human-verified) and ≤5% hallucination rate.
- Collect baseline data on student usage and staff time saved.
- Complete within $5,000 budget.

*Non-Goals:*
- Full university-wide rollout.
- Integration with existing LMS beyond basic web interface.
- Handling questions outside approved materials.
- Real-time chat or voice support.

**3. Stakeholder Map**

| Stakeholder | Role | Key Responsibility |
|-------------|------|-------------------|
| Faculty sponsor (part-time) | Approves materials, provides course context, final sign-off | Must approve materials by week 2 |
| Student developers (2) | Build and maintain the assistant | Primary builders; stipend $1,000 each |
| Teaching assistant (TA) | Tests answers, handles escalations, collects feedback | Essential for testing; $500 overtime |
| Course instructors (2) | Provide approved materials, review answer quality | Supply materials by week 2 |
| Students (≈300) | End users | Adoption depends on faculty promotion |
| Department head (informal) | Observes pilot, decides on expansion | Final decision-maker at week 12 |

**4. 12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Phase | Weeks | Tasks | Owner | Deliverables |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------------|
| Foundation | 1–2 | Define approved materials per course; set up version-controlled repo; select lightweight open-source LLM (e.g., Mistral 7B); draft privacy/academic-integrity safeguards | Faculty sponsor, developers | Approved material list; tech stack decision; safeguard document |
| Build | 3–5 | Ingest approved materials into vector database (e.g., Chroma); implement RAG pipeline; add hallucination guardrails (confidence threshold <70% → “I cannot answer” + escalate); build escalation button to TA | Developers | Working prototype with RAG pipeline; escalation interface |
| Test | 6–8 | Internal testing by TA and faculty sponsor; fix bugs; measure accuracy on 50 sample questions; refine guardrails; conduct small student focus group (n=10) | TA, faculty sponsor, developers | Test report with accuracy metrics; focus group feedback; updated prototype |
| Live operation | 9–12 | Deploy to both courses; monitor usage and accuracy; collect weekly student surveys; handle escalations; prepare final report | TA, developers, faculty sponsor | Weekly dashboards; final pilot report with metrics and recommendations |

**5. Resource and Budget Allocation**

*Total Budget: $5,000*

| Item | Cost | Justification |
|------|------|---------------|
| Cloud compute / API costs (weeks 1–12) | $2,000 | Lightweight open-source model on low-cost VM (~$50–$100/month) |
| Student developer stipends (2 × $1,000) | $2,000 | ~10 hours/week each for 12 weeks |
| TA overtime (testing & monitoring) | $500 | ~5 extra hours/week during weeks 6–12 |
| Contingency | $500 | For unexpected compute or API costs |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | |

*Assumptions:* Faculty sponsor and TA are already paid by university; no additional salary cost. Open-source tools (Chroma, FAISS) are free. University provides basic IT infrastructure (WiFi, authentication) at no extra cost.

**6. Risk Register with Mitigations**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination (answers not in approved materials) | Medium | High | RAG + confidence threshold <70% → refuse answer + escalate; mandatory source citation |
| Privacy breach (PII stored) | Low | High | No storage of PII; anonymized session logs only for improvement |
| Academic-integrity violation (assignment answers) | Medium | High | Assistant refuses to generate answers to assignments/exams; only clarifies concepts from approved materials |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Contingency fund; open-source tools; lightweight model |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Medium | Faculty promotion in class; TA outreach; weekly surveys |
| Faculty sponsor delay (materials not ready by week 2) | Medium | High | Clear deadline; backup plan to use sample materials from previous semester |

**7. Success Metrics and Data Collection Plan**

| Metric | Target | Data Collection Method | Frequency |
|--------|--------|------------------------|-----------|
| Answer accuracy | ≥85% | TA human-verification on 50 sample questions per week | Weekly during test and live phases |
| Hallucination rate | ≤5% | Automated logging of answers not grounded in approved materials | Continuous |
| Student satisfaction | ≥70% positive | Weekly survey (5-point scale) | Weekly during live phase |
| Escalation rate | ≤10% of total queries | Automated logging of escalation button use | Continuous |
| Staff time
